articles.title: Making 768 servers look like 1
articles.summary: Database sharding allows for the distribution of large amounts of data across multiple servers, making it easier to scale relational databases like Postgres or MySQL. By spreading queries and data out across many servers, database sharding can handle millions of queries per second with thousands of servers working together. This technology is essential for applications with millions of customers, as it enables the efficient storage and retrieval of large amounts of data.

articles.title: The Tokio/Rayon Trap and Why Async/Await Fails Concurrency
articles.summary: The Tokio/Rayon Trap and Why Async/Await Fails Concurrency because it hides control flow and obscures hardware realities, pushing developers back onto scheduling, which can lead to latency spikes and system crashes. This trap is exemplified by the "Compute Trap" in concurrent programming, where a CPU-bound task stalls the entire execution thread due to excessive I/O yield. By separating runtimes into I/O and CPU-bound tasks, developers can achieve better concurrency and avoid these issues.

articles.title: Detecting Full Table Scans With SQLite
articles.summary: Detecting Full Table Scans With SQLite is a technique that allows developers to detect whether a query has performed a full table scan on an SQLite database. By checking this value, developers can identify potential performance issues and potentially integrate this check into their application's testing framework.

articles.title: Abstracting effects with continuations
articles.summary: Representing errors as values is a powerful tool for writing reliable programs. A result value wraps the desired value or holds information about why the computation failed. An explicit result type represents the possibility of failure in the type system. Using continuations allows developers to generalize over the details of a computation and express any monad, including Result and Promise.

articles.title: SQLite should have (Rust-style) editions
articles.summary: SQLite's default foreign key constraints are often ignored by default, which can lead to dangling references and other issues in applications that rely on consistency. This is particularly problematic for projects where data integrity is crucial, such as embedded systems or financial software. The lack of enforced foreign key constraints also means that developers must manually manage user IDs and delete them when necessary, which can be error-prone and time-consuming.

It supports a context window of up to 1M tokens and was pretrained on 45 trillion tokens of text, images, audio, and video. The model balances cost with performance through efficient thinking effort and is not the strongest overall model available today, but its multimodal capabilities, efficient thinking, and availability on Tinker for fine-tuning make it a good open-weights base for customization.

articles.title: Microsoft Confirms Windows GDID Device Identifier That Cannot Be Disabled, Documented in FBI Case Filing
articles.summary: Microsoft has confirmed that it assigns a unique device-specific ID to each Windows installation using its Global Device Identifier (GDID), which cannot be disabled without affecting Windows activation and Microsoft Store apps. The GDID is generated by a chain of services, including wlidsvc, login.live.com, and the Connected Devices Platform, and is stored in the Windows registry under HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\IdentityCRL\ExtendedProperties. This identifier has been used by law enforcement to track alleged Scattered Spider hacking group member Peter Stokes across various networks and locations over a period of eight months.

articles.title: a bunch of stuff i used to not know about K&R C
articles.summary: C's grammar is context-sensitive and ambiguous due to its single-pass compiler design, which requires tracking scopes and identifiers while parsing. This was a necessity for heavily memory-constrained systems like those in the original C compiler. The fact that identifiers can refer to typedefs doesn't matter since it's not much extra work to check; however, modern C's scoping rules are particularly problematic due to its discontinuous scope rules. Void is an ANSI invention, likely introduced as a way to simplify function declarations without requiring explicit return types.
